text: The patient is positioned supine for a laparoscopic abdominoperineal resection. The surgical team is arranged with the surgeon and cameraman on the right side and the assistant on the left. A five-port technique is utilized, including a 10mm main camera port and right-hand port, along with three 5mm ports. The abdominal cavity is thoroughly visualized using monopolar cautery, confirming operability.

The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory and pulls the sigmoid colon to make the sigmoid mesentery taut. The mesentery is opened, and the internal incision is extended into the pelvis, stopping near the middle of the right ureter. The inferior mesenteric vessels are retracted anteriorly, and the correct dissection plane is identified between the vessels and the ureter, with precautions taken to avoid injury to the ureter and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon continues dissection until reaching the source muscles, marking the lateral limit of the dissection. The mesenteric artery is identified at its root, skeletonized, and clipped. Using a ligature, the inferior mesenteric artery is cut, followed by the identification and cutting of the inferior mesenteric vein. The holy plane is identified between the fascia propria and pre-sacral fascia, where loose areolar tissue is visible, allowing dissection without bleeding.

The surgeon applies anterior traction on the rectum while dissecting the pre-sacral fascia posteriorly. The sigmoid colon is fully mobilized by incising the peritoneum lateral to it. The left ureter is identified, and dissection continues medial to the ureter, reaching the previously created plane.

The surgeon performs left lateral dissection by retracting the rectum towards the right side and incising the peritoneum. Continuous traction reveals the left lateral TMA plane between the mesorectum and pelvic fascia. The pelvic nerves, situated deep to the pelvic fascia, are not visible, emphasizing the importance of staying in the correct plane to avoid nerve injury. Following the completion of left lateral dissection, the surgeon begins the right lateral dissection.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum on the right side, identifying the TME plane between the mesorectum and the right lateral pelvic fascia, an avascular area. Anterior dissection begins with the identification of the rectovesicle pouch and seminal vesicle. Posteriorly, the holy plane is situated between the seminal vesicle anteriorly and Denonvilliers' fascia posteriorly, with dissection occurring between the right seminal vesicle and the fascia, ensuring no bleeding by remaining in the correct plane.

The surgeon dissects between the left seminal vesicle and the fascia, ensuring pelvic nerves are safeguarded by staying in the correct plane. The dissection continues between the prostate and Denonvilliers' fascia, keeping the fascia with the specimen. Lateral and posterior dissection proceeds digitally until the fibers of the levator muscle are visible, marking the limit of the dissection.

The surgeon identifies the point of colonic transection and cuts the mesocolon. The colon is transected and brought out as an end stoma. The surgical area is irrigated, hemostasis is achieved, and a drain is placed. Finally, a colostomy bag is applied.
